description: Optical micrographs comparing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels as a function of aluminum content after granulation and pressing at 1150°C followed by quenching from 1100°C. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure of steel containing 9.8% Al, exhibiting a homogeneous austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains and absence of δ-ferrite. Subfigure (b) displays the microstructure of steel with 14% Al, revealing the presence of δ-ferrite (labeled as 'δ' with an arrow indicating the phase) as a secondary phase formed along grain boundaries or within the matrix. The accompanying citation states that δ-ferrite reaches approximately 10% volume fraction at 14% Al, whereas rapid solidification (jet spraying) can suppress δ-ferrite formation up to 12% Al. These images demonstrate the critical role of aluminum content in promoting embrittling δ-ferrite formation, supporting the definition of composition limits (Al < 9.7% for optimum microstructure) for structural applications requiring UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and impact toughness > 50 J/cm². Both images share a 30 μm scale bar.
